hypotheses.title: AI-Assisted FortiGate Compromise via Weak Credentials and Exposed Ports
hypotheses.description: A Russian-speaking threat actor is using commercial generative AI tools to automate the scanning and compromise of internet-exposed FortiGate firewalls. The attacker is not exploiting zero-days but is abusing exposed management ports (443, 8443) and weak single-factor credentials to gain administrative access, potentially leading to lateral movement or data exfiltration.
mitre_attack_techniques: T1021.001 - Remote Services: Remote Desktop Protocol
T1059.001 - Command and Scripting Interpreter: PowerShell

data_sources_needed.specific_data: Firewall logs, NetFlow data, and authentication logs from FortiGate devices
EDR logs showing PowerShell execution or suspicious process spawning on management workstations

hunting_tactics.description: Identify successful logins to FortiGate management interfaces from unusual geolocations or IP ranges, especially those followed by configuration changes.
sample_queries: Splunk SPL: index=fortigate event_type=login status=success | stats count by src_ip, user, _time | where count > 5 | table src_ip, user, count
KQL: FortiGateLogs | where EventID == "LoginSuccess" | summarize count() by SrcIP, User | where count_ > 3
hunting_tactics.look_for: Multiple failed logins followed by a success, logins from non-corporate IP ranges, or logins occurring outside business hours.

hunting_tactics.description: Search for PowerShell or shell scripts executed on management workstations that contain patterns typical of AI-generated reconnaissance or credential harvesting.
sample_queries: Splunk SPL: index=edr ProcessName="powershell.exe" | search "Invoke-WebRequest" OR "Get-Content" OR "Invoke-RestMethod" | stats count by CommandLine
KQL: DeviceProcessEvents | where ProcessName == "powershell.exe" | where CommandLine has_any ("Invoke-WebRequest", "Get-Content", "Invoke-RestMethod")
hunting_tactics.look_for: Execution of scripts that query external APIs, download files from unknown URLs, or attempt to enumerate network devices.

network_patterns: Outbound connections to unknown IPs from management workstations
other_indicators: Repeated failed login attempts on FortiGate
Configuration changes to firewall rules or admin accounts

recommended_actions: Immediately rotate credentials for all FortiGate management accounts.
Restrict management access to specific IP ranges via firewall rules.
Enable MFA on all FortiGate management interfaces.
Isolate compromised devices and perform a full forensic analysis.

hypotheses.title: ClickFix Campaigns Abusing Windows Terminal for Lumma Stealer Deployment
hypotheses.description: Threat actors are evolving the ClickFix social engineering technique by instructing users to launch Windows Terminal (via Win+X -> I) instead of the Run dialog. Users are then tricked into pasting malicious PowerShell commands that deploy the Lumma Stealer, bypassing traditional Run-dialog detections.
mitre_attack_techniques: T1059.001 - Command and Scripting Interpreter: PowerShell

data_sources_needed.specific_data: Process creation logs, PowerShell script block logs, and command-line arguments
Email headers and body content for social engineering lures

hunting_tactics.description: Detect the specific shortcut sequence (Win+X -> I) or direct launches of wt.exe followed immediately by PowerShell execution.
sample_queries: Splunk SPL: index=edr ProcessName="wt.exe" | search "wt.exe" | stats count by CommandLine, ParentProcessName | where ParentProcessName != "explorer.exe"

KQL: DeviceProcessEvents | where ProcessName == "wt.exe" | where CommandLine has "wt.exe" | join kind=inner (DeviceProcessEvents | where ProcessName == "powershell.exe" | where Timestamp > start_time) on DeviceId
hunting_tactics.look_for: wt.exe spawning powershell.exe with arguments containing encoded commands or downloading files.

hunting_tactics.description: Identify outbound connections to known Lumma Stealer C2 infrastructure or unusual data exfiltration patterns.
sample_queries: Splunk SPL: index=network | search dest_ip IN ("185.220.101.*", "194.26.192.*") OR dest_domain IN ("lumma-stealer-c2.com", "malicious-domain.net")
KQL: NetworkTraffic | where DestinationIp in ("185.220.101.*", "194.26.192.*")
hunting_tactics.look_for: Connections to newly registered domains, high-volume data exfiltration to unknown IPs, or connections from browser processes to non-standard ports.

network_patterns: Outbound HTTPS to unknown IPs from browser processes
DNS queries for newly registered domains
other_indicators: PowerShell commands containing 'Invoke-WebRequest' or 'DownloadString' pasted into terminal

recommended_actions: Block execution of PowerShell from Windows Terminal unless explicitly whitelisted.
Deploy EDR rules to detect the specific command-line arguments used in ClickFix campaigns.
Conduct user awareness training on the new Windows Terminal variant of ClickFix.
Scan endpoints for Lumma Stealer indicators and isolate infected systems.

hypotheses.title: Supply Chain Compromise via Malicious NPM Packages (SANDWORM_MODE)
hypotheses.description: A new supply chain worm campaign, codenamed SANDWORM_MODE, is using typosquatted NPM packages to infect developer machines and CI/CD pipelines. The malware harvests secrets, API tokens, and crypto keys, and can modify other repositories using stolen credentials, posing a significant risk to software development environments.
mitre_attack_techniques: T1195.002 - Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Software Supply Chain
T1562.001 - Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

data_sources_needed.specific_data: CI/CD pipeline logs, NPM registry logs, and cloud access logs
Node.js process logs, package installation logs, and environment variable access

hunting_tactics.description: Monitor for the installation of NPM packages with names similar to popular libraries but with slight variations (typosquatting).
sample_queries: Splunk SPL: index=ci-cd | search "npm install" | regex "npm install [a-z]+-[a-z]+" | stats count by package_name
KQL: PackageInstallationLogs | where PackageName matches regex "^[a-z]+-[a-z]+$" | summarize count() by PackageName
hunting_tactics.look_for: Installation of packages with names like 'react-dom', 'lodash', 'axios' but with slight misspellings or extra characters.

hunting_tactics.description: Detect attempts to access or exfiltrate environment variables, API keys, or secrets from CI/CD pipelines.
sample_queries: Splunk SPL: index=ci-cd | search "process.env" OR "process.argv" | stats count by job_id, user
KQL: CI_CD_Logs | where Message has "process.env" | summarize count() by JobId, User
hunting_tactics.look_for: Scripts accessing environment variables like GITHUB_TOKEN, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY, or NPM_TOKEN and sending them to external endpoints.

network_patterns: Outbound connections from CI/CD runners to unknown IPs
other_indicators: Modification of repository files by CI/CD jobs

recommended_actions: Audit all NPM dependencies for typosquatting and known malicious packages.
Rotate all CI/CD tokens and API keys immediately.
Implement strict allowlists for NPM packages in CI/CD pipelines.
Scan repositories for unauthorized modifications.

hypotheses.title: OAuth Redirect Abuse for Malware Delivery
hypotheses.description: Attackers are abusing the legitimate OAuth redirection mechanism to bypass email and browser defenses. They craft URLs with popular identity providers (Microsoft Entra ID, Google Workspace) that redirect victims to attacker-controlled infrastructure, delivering malware without stealing tokens.
mitre_attack_techniques: T1071.001 - Application Layer Protocol: Web Protocols

data_sources_needed.specific_data: Email logs containing OAuth redirect URLs
Web proxy logs, DNS logs, and HTTP request logs

hunting_tactics.description: Identify OAuth URLs that contain redirect parameters pointing to non-standard or suspicious domains.
sample_queries: Splunk SPL: index=email | search "login.microsoftonline.com" OR "accounts.google.com" | regex "redirect_uri=[^&]*" | stats count by redirect_uri
KQL: EmailLogs | where MessageBody has "redirect_uri" | summarize count() by redirect_uri
hunting_tactics.look_for: Redirect URIs that do not match the expected domain of the identity provider or point to known malicious domains.

hunting_tactics.description: Detect downloads or executions of malware following an OAuth redirect event.
sample_queries: Splunk SPL: index=proxy | search "login.microsoftonline.com" | stats count by dest_ip, dest_port
KQL: NetworkTraffic | where DestinationUrl has "login.microsoftonline.com" | join kind=inner (DeviceProcessEvents | where ProcessName == "powershell.exe") on DeviceId
hunting_tactics.look_for: Outbound connections to unknown IPs immediately after an OAuth redirect, or execution of PowerShell scripts from browser processes.

network_patterns: HTTP 302 redirects from OAuth providers to unknown domains
other_indicators: OAuth URLs with 'error' or 'redirect_uri' parameters pointing to suspicious domains

recommended_actions: Configure email security gateways to block OAuth URLs with suspicious redirect parameters.
Implement browser policies to restrict OAuth redirects to trusted domains.
Monitor for unusual OAuth activity and investigate any redirects to unknown domains.
Educate users on the risks of clicking OAuth links in emails.

hypotheses.title: OpenClaw AI Agent Hijacking via Localhost WebSocket Exploitation
hypotheses.description: A critical vulnerability (ClawJacked) in the OpenClaw AI agent framework allows malicious websites to connect to the local OpenClaw gateway via WebSocket, brute-force passwords, and hijack the agent. This enables attackers to steal data, execute commands, and gain persistent access to the victim's machine.
mitre_attack_techniques: T1059.001 - Command and Scripting Interpreter: PowerShell

data_sources_needed.specific_data: Process creation logs, network connection logs, and WebSocket traffic logs
Localhost traffic logs, WebSocket connection logs

hunting_tactics.description: Detect unexpected WebSocket connections to localhost ports used by OpenClaw or similar AI agents.
sample_queries: Splunk SPL: index=network | search "localhost" AND "WebSocket" | stats count by src_ip, dest_port
KQL: NetworkTraffic | where DestinationIp == "127.0.0.1" | where Protocol == "WebSocket" | summarize count() by DestinationPort
hunting_tactics.look_for: WebSocket connections to localhost ports (e.g., 8080, 3000) from browser processes or unknown applications.

hunting_tactics.description: Identify processes spawned by OpenClaw that execute suspicious commands or access sensitive files.
sample_queries: Splunk SPL: index=edr | search "openclaw" | stats count by CommandLine, ParentProcessName
KQL: DeviceProcessEvents | where ProcessName has "openclaw" | summarize count() by CommandLine
hunting_tactics.look_for: OpenClaw processes executing PowerShell scripts, accessing browser data, or connecting to external C2 servers.

other_indicators: Unauthorized access to browser data or files

recommended_actions: Immediately update OpenClaw to the latest patched version (2026.2.26 or later).
Block all WebSocket connections to localhost ports used by AI agents unless explicitly required.
Monitor for unauthorized access to browser data or sensitive files by AI agent processes.
Conduct a full forensic analysis of systems running OpenClaw.

unique_mitre_techniques: T1195.002 - Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Software Supply Chain
T1059.001 - Command and Scripting Interpreter: PowerShell
T1021.001 - Remote Services: Remote Desktop Protocol
T1071.001 - Application Layer Protocol: Web Protocols
T1562.001 - Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools
